data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Expected to Stay Rangebound Near Term — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. Treasury yields seem likely to remain constrained in a narrow range to start the new year as the U.S. economy continues to expand, says Federated Hermes' RJ Gallo in a note. Consumer spending, supported by higher income households and corporate capital expenditures linked to the AI build-out, continues to provide a solid foundation for U.S. growth, the deputy chief CIO for global fixed income says. While markets face incomplete and delayed data releases in the wake of the government shutdown, private sector and alternative data sources continue to portray a resilient U.S. economy and inflation remains closer to 3% than to the Federal Reserve's 2% target, he says. Meanwhile, despite the economy's resilience, job creation has slowed, and corporate layoff announcements have increased, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: POSCO Future Inks MoU With Factorial To Develop All-Solid-State Battery Materials
data.description:
SEOUL (dpa-AFX) - POSCO Future M Co., Ltd.(003670.KS), a South Korean battery materials provider, said on Friday that it has inked a memorandum of understanding with Factorial Inc., a solid-state battery technology company, to develop all-solid-state battery technology.

Hong Young-Jun, Head of Technology Research Laboratory at POSCO Future M, said: 'We expect synergy in the next-generation all-solid-state battery business based on Factorial's battery technology and market presence with global automakers, and POSCO Future M's competitiveness in cathode and anode materials.'

The two companies will plan to explore cooperation in developing materials for all-solid-state batteries, which are powering progress in the industries such as electric vehicles, robotics, and others.

All-solid-state batteries use solid electrolytes instead of liquid between the cathode and anode, to provide higher safety, superior energy density, and excellent charging performance compared with conventional lithium-ion batteries.

data.title: Swiss Re: Buyback Subject to Reaching 2025 Net Income Target of More Than $4.4B
Buy Prestige Estates; target of Rs 2295: Motilal Oswal
data.description:
Motilal Oswal's research report on Prestige Estates

Prestige Estates (PEPL) has a diverse portfolio with a presence in the residential, office, retail, and hospitality segments. The company’s 1HFY26 incremental BD of INR331b and a launch pipeline of INR770b are expected to drive a presales CAGR of 40% over FY25-28, reaching INR463b by FY28. PEPL is expanding its office & retail segment to 50msf and scaling up its hospitality portfolio. As a result, office and retail rental income is likely to cumulatively clock a 53% CAGR to reach INR25.1b, while hospitality revenue is expected to post a 22% CAGR over FY25-28, reaching INR16.0b. Commercial income is projected to improve to INR33b by FY30E as all underconstruction assets become fully operational. PEPL has rapidly gained market share in MMR, entered the NCR market with significant traction, and is also scaling up in Pune, which will generate incremental income streams.

Outlook

Therefore, we remain highly confident in PEPL’s growth prospects and reiterate our BUY rating with a TP of INR2,295.

data.title: South Africa Forex Reserves Post Historical High
data.description:
Gross foreign exchange reserves in South Africa rose to a new historical high of $72.07 billion in November 2025 from $71.55 billion in the previous month.

Increases were recorded in gold reserves ($16.838 billion vs $16.210 billion in October) and SDR holdings ($6.502 billion vs $6.501 billion).

On the other hand, foreign currency reserves posted a decrease ($48.728 billion vs $48.839 billion), while the forward position, representing the central bank’s unsettled or swap transactions, fell to $0.552 billion in November from $0.602 in the preceding period.

data.title: Swiss Re targets net income of $4.5 billion in 2026
data.description:
Swiss Re announced its targets for 2026, saying it is aiming for group net income of $4.5 billion, the world's second-largest reinsurer said ahead of an investor day on Friday.

The figure was less than the $4.81 billion expected by analysts according to a consensus compiled by Visible Alpha.

data.title: European Economic News Preview: German Factory Orders Data Due
data.description:
PRAG (dpa-AFX) - Factory orders from Germany and revised GDP from the euro area are due on Friday, headlining a busy day for the European economic news.

At 2.00 am ET, Destatis is scheduled to issue Germany's factory orders data for October. Orders are forecast to climb 0.3 percent month-on-month, slower than the 1.1 percent increase seen in September.

In the meantime, UK Halifax house price data is due. Economists expect house prices to rise 0.2 percent on a monthly basis in November, following a 0.6 percent increase in October.

At 2.45 am ET, France's industrial production data is due. Production is forecast to fall 0.1 percent month-on-month in October, in contrast to the 0.8 percent rise in September.

At 3.00 am ET, industrial output data is due from Spain. In the meantime, retail sales from the Czech Republic and foreign trade from Austria are due.

At 4.00 am ET, Italy's statistical office ISTAT is scheduled to release retail sales for October. Sales are forecast to climb 0.4 percent month-on-month, reversing a 0.5 percent drop in the previous month.

At 5.00 am ET, Eurostat is slated to publish euro area revised GDP data for the third quarter. The initial estimate showed that the economy expanded 0.2 percent sequentially after rising 0.1 percent a quarter ago.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Expected to Stay Rangebound Near Term — Market Talk
data.description:
0612 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields seem likely to remain constrained in a narrow range to start the new year as the U.S. economy continues to expand, says Federated Hermes' RJ Gallo in a note. Consumer spending, supported by higher income households and corporate capital expenditures linked to the AI build-out, continues to provide a solid foundation for U.S. growth, the deputy chief CIO for global fixed income says. While markets face incomplete and delayed data releases in the wake of the government shutdown, private sector and alternative data sources continue to portray a resilient U.S. economy and inflation remains closer to 3% than to the Federal Reserve's 2% target, he says. Meanwhile, despite the economy's resilience, job creation has slowed, and corporate layoff announcements have increased, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0521 GMT - At its policy meeting next Tuesday, the Reserve Bank of Australia will likely express caution about reading too much into new monthly inflation data that showed a further rise in price pressures, says Shane Oliver, chief economist at AMP. But the RBA is also likely to reiterate its concerns about capacity constraints and warn that if higher inflation proves more persistent then it might have implications for interest rates, he adds. It's also likely to state that it will do whatever is necessary to bring inflation back to target, leaving the door wide open for a rate increase next year, Oliver says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0511 GMT - The Reserve Bank of Australia's historical reaction function in prior interest-rate hiking cycles is arguably already met, says George Tharenou, chief economist for Australia at UBS. In prior cash rate hiking cycles the RBA started to increase rates when headline CPI started to pick up, he adds. In more recent hiking cycles, the RBA started to raise rates when the trimmed mean measure of core inflation was around 3.0% on year. Based on the RBA's historical reaction function to inflation outcomes, this means the trigger to hike rates has arguably already been met, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0458 GMT - AUD/USD is topping the weekly FX leaderboard as a hawkish repricing of interest rate expectations continues, says Richard Franulovich, head of FX strategy at Westpac. The Australian dollar is enjoying a degree of yield support that hasn't been in place for years, he adds. There is some wariness that the repricing has gone too far: 11 bps in rate-hike risk is priced for May next year, he adds. RBA Gov. Michele Bullock has plenty of reasons to continue to sound incrementally more hawkish, he says. Westpac is targeting a retest of the 0.6707 September highs. The pair now trades at 0.6620. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0434 GMT - The Philippine peso has weakened in recent months, and UOB says it could still underperform its regional peers next year, as domestic factors will likely outweigh expectations for broad dollar weakness. The Philippines is facing headwinds such as Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas' easing cycle, fiscal credibility concerns and deteriorating economic fundamentals, UOB's Global Economics and Markets Research team says in a report. Political uncertainties and tariff headwinds have also been weighing on investment sentiment, consumer confidence and exports. UOB forecasts USD/PHP at 58.2 in 1Q, 57.8 in 2Q, 57.4 in 3Q and 57.0 by 4Q. USD/PHP is up 0.2% at 58.958, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0423 GMT - China is likely to maintain its economic growth target at around 5% again next year, Macquarie economists write in a note. Since this year's growth target can be met with limited government stimulus, policymakers are likely to consider the 5% growth quite achievable and set the same target for next year, they say. Exports are likely to remain the main tailwind with the economists expecting an export growth of 6% next year, due to accelerating global growth, easing U.S.-China trade tensions and rising Chinese manufacturing competitiveness, they add. Deflationary pressure is likely to persist due to a lack of credit upcycle, they say. The brokerage estimates that consumer inflation will rise only 0.3% and producer prices will fall by 1.8% next year.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0359 GMT - Indonesia's trade faces significant headwinds in 2026, UOB Kay Hian analyst Suryaputra Wijaksana writes in a research note. That stems from a global slowdown and protectionist policies targeting Indonesia's key exports, such as coal and palm oil. Efforts by India, a major buyer of palm oil, to reduce imports of the commodity, threaten Indonesia. However, these challenges could be partially offset by potential new trade agreements with the U.S. and demand from Europe, the analyst says. A tariff-free trade deal with the U.S. would provide a modest boost to noncommodity exports, he adds. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0244 GMT - The Singapore dollar is steady against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session, as market participants await the Fed's rate decision next week. Newly filed unemployment claims in the U.S. last week dropped to the lowest level in three years, the Labor Department said Thursday. Signs of a softer U.S. labor market reinforce market expectations of a Fed rate cut, Commerzbank Research analysts write in a note. USD/SGD is little changed at 1.2955, LSEG data shows.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0243 GMT - Beijing may opt to target a lower growth range of 4.5%-5.0% for 2026 from around 5.0% this year owing to challenges ahead, especially the property-sector fallout, rising trade tensions and an aging population, Nomura Research analysts say. The growth target is expected to be discussed at the Central Economic Work Conference later this month, though it won't be released until March 2026. Beijing could maintain the official fiscal deficit ratio at 4.0% of GDP, while the central government special bonds quota may be raised to 1.6 trillion yuan in 2026 from 1.3 trillion yuan in 2025, they say. The analysts expect the CEWC to confirm an extension of the trade-in program, but add that most of the 2026 consumption measures will likely be a continuation of 2025 policies. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0218 GMT - The Bank of Japan is expected to keep increasing its policy rate every six months after raising the target to 0.75% from 0.5% at its Dec. 18-19 meeting, writes Bank of America economist Takayasu Kudo. "Based on recent developments in corporate earnings, wage negotiations, the yen's depreciation in the FX market, and dialogue with the government, it has become increasingly clear that the BOJ is gaining confidence in raising rates at the upcoming December meeting," he says in a note. The economist expects the BOJ to raise rates in June 2026, followed by hikes in January and July 2027. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0151 GMT - JGB yields have been under upward pressure amid expectations for the Bank of Japan's December rate hike, and analysts recommend investors brace for further yield rises. "At least from an economic fundamentals perspective, the current 10-year bond yield is not excessively high," says SMBC Nikko Securities strategist Ataru Okumura. "If the 10-year yield rises to 2% at a rapid pace like we have been seeing, we should remain cautious about further yield increases from that point." The benchmark 10-year JGB yield hit 1.940% Friday morning, the highest level since July 2007.(megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0111 GMT - The 10-year Japanese government bond yields hits an 18-year high, driven by continued expectations for the Bank of Japan's rate increase later this month as well as by persistently high inflation. Investors are focusing on economic indicators to gauge the strength of the economy, including revised July-September gross domestic product data due Monday. Government data showed earlier Friday that household spending fell 3.0% in October from a year earlier, undershooting a 1.0% rise expected. The 10-year JGB yield is up half a basis point at 1.940%, its highest level since July 2007, while the 2-year yield is flat at 1.020%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: Swiss Re: Dividend Per Share Growth Target Is for Next 2 Years
India Central Bank Delivers Rate Cut to Boost Economy — Update
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

India's central bank cut its key policy rate, snapping a two-meeting run of pauses as cooling inflation creates room to lend the economy more support against tariff risks.

The Reserve Bank of India on Friday voted unanimously to lower its policy repo rate to 5.25% from 5.50%. The RBI also voted to keep its monetary policy stance at neutral.

"The growth-inflation balance, especially the benign inflation outlook on both headline and core, continues to provide the policy space to support the growth momentum," said RBI Gov. Sanjay Malhotra.

Most economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal had expected the RBI to resume easing, despite India's economy outperforming expectations in the last quarter.

That's largely because while many countries have negotiated trade pacts with the Trump administration, an India-U.S. agreement remains in limbo. That puts Asia's third-largest economy at a disadvantage, with its exporters facing hefty 50% tariffs--far above those set on its peers.

"Growth, while remaining resilient, is expected to soften somewhat," Malhotra said.

"External uncertainties continue to pose downside risks to the outlook, while speedy conclusion of various ongoing trade and investment negotiations present upside potential," Malhotra said. Services exports are likely to remain strong, but merchandise exports face some headwinds, he added.

On the domestic side, the RBI expects factors such as benign inflation and continued impact of goods-and-services tax rationalization to support economic activity.

The RBI raised its real gross domestic product growth forecast for this fiscal year ending March to 7.3% from 6.8% previously. It cut its full-year consumer inflation outlook to 2.0% from its forecast in October of 2.6%.

If the hit from U.S. tariffs hobbles growth significantly, that could prompt the RBI to act again, analysts say.

The rupee, which has been plumbing new lows against the dollar in part because of tariff concerns, stood at 89.87 to the dollar after the decision. It crossed the psychologically important level of 90 this week.

The RBI also said it will buy bonds worth 1 trillion rupees, the equivalent to $11.13 billion, and will conduct a three-year USD/INR buy-sell swap of $5 billion this month to inject durable liquidity into the system.

The RBI could deliver another 25-basis-point rate cut in February, given that inflation is expected to remain subdued and growth is likely to cool in the near term as the drag from U.S. tariffs play out, Capital Economics said in a note.

"Although inflation is likely to rise over the coming year as base effects turn less favorable, we think a return to target is unlikely before late 2026, barring an agricultural supply shock," Capital Economics said.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com
